title: Cream horns
subtitle: Puff pastry cones filled with whipped cream and raspberry jam
excerpt: Strips of puff pastry wound around conical metal moulds and baked until shatteringly crisp, then filled with whipped cream over a dab of raspberry jam. These are a classic British bakery item. The pastry must be kept cold throughout for the lamination to puff properly. They are filled just before serving. The whole process takes about 1 hour.
sourceNotes: Cream horns have been a staple of the British high street bakery since at least the 1920s and appear in Constance Spry's 'The Constance Spry Cookery Book' (1956) and in later editions of Mrs Beeton. The technique of winding pastry around metal moulds is documented in continental European pastry manuals from the nineteenth century. The same technique produces ice cream cones in commercial settings.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The baked, unfilled horns keep in an airtight tin for 24 hours. Fill just before serving; filled horns go soft quickly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; keep the pastry cold right until it goes into the oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Puff pastry lifts because water in the butter layers turns to steam in a hot oven, forcing the layers apart. If the butter melts before the oven's heat sets the surrounding dough, the layers collapse together and the pastry becomes dense and greasy. Keeping puff pastry cold throughout preparation means the butter stays solid until it enters the oven. For cream horns, chill the wound moulds for 20 minutes before baking; this gives the gluten time to relax and ensures the butter is as cold as possible at the moment of baking.

content.text: The single most important rule for cream horns is
until the moment it goes into the oven. Work quickly when cutting and winding; if the pastry warms up, refrigerate the moulds for 20 minutes before baking.

The cream horns are filled just before serving: once filled, the cream migrates into the pastry within an hour and the pastry loses its crispness. You can bake the horns up to 24 hours ahead and store them in an airtight tin.

Unroll the cold puff pastry sheet. Cut into 8 strips, each about 2.5 cm wide and 25 cm long. Grease 8 cream horn moulds lightly. Starting at the point, wind a strip of pastry around each mould in a tight spiral, overlapping each turn by about 5 mm. Place on a lined baking tray. Refrigerate for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Brush the chilled pastry horns with beaten egg and sprinkle with granulated sugar. Bake for 16 to 20 minutes until the pastry is deep golden brown and puffed, and the layers are visibly separated. Slide off the moulds while still warm; if left to cool on the moulds they will contract and stick. Cool on a rack.

Just before serving, whip the 300 ml cold double cream with 2 tbsp icing sugar and 0.5 tsp vanilla to soft peaks. Put a half-teaspoon of raspberry jam into the pointed base of each horn, then fill with the cream using a piping bag or a spoon, starting at the tip and working toward the wide opening.

items.problem: Pastry did not puff and looks greasy
items.solution: The butter in the pastry melted before the oven's heat set the layers. Ensure the wound horns are refrigerated for the full 20 minutes before baking, and that the oven is fully at 220 degrees C when they go in.

items.problem: Pastry stuck to the moulds when trying to slide them off
items.solution: The moulds were not greased lightly before winding, or the horns cooled fully on the moulds. Slide them off while still warm; a gentle twisting motion as you pull helps release them.

items.problem: Pastry turned soft quickly after filling
items.solution: Fill no more than 30 minutes before serving. The cream and jam introduce moisture that will soften the pastry over time regardless of how crisp it was when it came out of the oven.
